data.title: Poll finds more than half oppose Trump ballroom following WHCA assassination attempt
data.description:
A new poll conducted by the Washington Post, ABC News and Ipsos found that more than half of respondents oppose President Trump’s plan to construct a ballroom on the White House grounds.

The survey, released Thursday, found that 56 percent of respondents are opposed to the project, which began in October 2025 with the teardown of the East Wing.

Just under 3 in 10 respondents, meanwhile, said they support the construction of the ballroom, while 16 percent saying they were unsure or skipped the question. 

The shares of those who oppose, support or were unsure or skipped the question were identical to a Washington Post-ABC News-Ipsos poll conducted in October. 

The latest survey was conducted from last Friday through Tuesday. On Saturday, an armed man charged a security checkpoint one floor above where the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner was taking place.

The man exchanged shots with law enforcement, but did not reach the floor where Trump, administration officials, members of Congress and journalists were gathered. Cole Allen, 31, was charged Monday with attempting to assassinate the president and other firearms violations. 

The morning after the incident, Trump doubled down on his support for the ballroom project — arguing it is needed for security reasons. 

“What happened last night is exactly the reason that our great Military, Secret Service, Law Enforcement and, for different reasons, every President for the last 150 years, have been DEMANDING that a large, safe, and secure Ballroom be built ON THE GROUNDS OF THE WHITE HOUSE,” the president wrote on his Truth Social platform. “This event would never have happened with the Militarily Top Secret Ballroom currently under construction at the White House.”

While Trump said in October that the ballroom project would be funded via $300 million in private donations, a group of Senate Republicans this week introduced a bill to authorize $400 million in taxpayer dollars to pay for the structure.

Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) and other GOP senators said that the cost of the project would be offset by national park user fees and customs fees, and that the bill would authorize the construction of national security-related infrastructure below the ballroom, including an annex for the Secret Service. 

Yet Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) told reporters Tuesday that the legislation would have to wait amid negotiations on funding the Department of Homeland Security.

The latest Washington Post-ABC News-Ipsos poll was conducted via online interviews with 1,292 U.S. adults. It has a margin of error of 2.8 percentage points.

data.title: Watch live: Trump holds farewell ceremony for King Charles, Queen Camilla
data.description:
President Trump and first lady Melania Trump will host King Charles III and Queen Camilla at the White House for a farewell ceremony on Thursday morning.

The president, who met with Charles on Tuesday in the Oval Office, praised the British monarch during the visit and lauded his support for stopping Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon. The king and queen also joined the Trumps for a state dinner earlier this week.

Charles also gave a speech before a joint meeting of Congress, calling for more international cooperation.

The king and queen traveled to New York City on Wednesday and are set to visit Arlington National Cemetery outside of Washington, D.C. in the afternoon and will participate in a parade in Front Royal, Va., later Thursday.

The event is scheduled to begin at 10:50 a.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: I've dressed a million girls for prom; this year, tariffs are pricing them out
data.description:
My father came to New York with almost nothing. He got a job in the Garment District, learned the business from the floor up, and in 1980 he opened a small dress shop with 10 people and fifteen styles. That was Jovani.

He believed — and I mean this simply and without any marketing behind it — that every woman deserves to feel beautiful.

My brother Saul and I grew up in that belief. We built our careers around it. We still run the company the same way he did: From New York, in the Garment District, personally involved in every decision.

To this day, Jovani donates tens of thousands of dresses each year to young women who would not otherwise be able to afford a milestone moment. This has never been about a campaign, but rather a belief my father held that every woman deserves to feel beautiful, confident, and celebrated, regardless of her circumstances.

So, when I tell you that what is happening right now with tariffs and trade policy is going to hurt the girls shopping for prom dresses this spring, I need you to understand that I am not speaking as a business owner protecting margins. I am speaking as someone who has spent 40 years making sure the price of a dress is never what stands in the way of a girl’s magic moment.

Here is what is actually happening. This year, U.S. apparel tariffs have shifted rapidly. A set of emergency tariffs was struck down by the Supreme Court, only to be replaced days later with a new 10 percent surcharge under a different authority, with the administration signaling a push toward the 15 percent ceiling.

Today, apparel tariffs average around 13 percent, with no clarity on what comes next. But for special-occasion dresses, where layered duties and sourcing differences drive higher costs, the reality is far more significant. Once surcharges and additional duties are factored in, effective tariffs can range from roughly 17 percent to as high as 34 percent, depending on fabric and country of origin. In many cases, they are now well above 25 percent, costs that ultimately reach families at the register.

Nobody in Washington is talking about prom dresses. They are talking about semiconductors and steel. But the policy lands on a 17-year-old in Kansas City whose mom budgeted $500, or who has saved from that part time job she works on the weekends. It lands on the boutique owner in Atlanta who ordered inventory six months ago under one set of rules and is now selling it under another.

It lands on brands like ours that are trying to absorb as much of the increase as possible rather than pass it directly to the customer — which is not something most companies can sustain indefinitely.

Washington may be debating policy at a high level, but the impact is anything but abstract. It shows up in small businesses forced to make impossible pricing decisions, and in families quietly recalculating what they can afford. That reality deserves more attention, because it is not just economic, it is deeply personal and it’s impacting memories that are supposed to last a lifetime. It is the reason some girls will not be able to afford the dress they dreamed of for prom because of government policy.

Prom season is underway right now. The dresses are on the racks. Families are making decisions this week. And somewhere today, a girl is trying on a dress that fits perfectly while her mother quietly checks the price tag and recalculates whether it’s even possible.

In that moment, policy stops being abstract.

Abraham Maslavi is co-Founder and CFO of Jovani Fashions, a New York City-based eveningwear brand founded in 1983. Jovani distributes through more than 2,000 retailers worldwide and donates more than 30,000 dresses annually to women facing financial hardship.

data.title: Watch live: Senate Democrats highlight Iran war powers resolution push
data.description:
Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) and Sen. Adam Schiff (D-Calif.) will speak with reporters Thursday morning as Democrats prepare to force another vote on a war powers resolution to block the Trump administration from further action in Iran.

Thursday’s vote marks the sixth attempt and comes as the conflict nears a key milestone in the War Powers Act: 60 days. Some Republicans have signaled that threshold could be a turning point in their support for ongoing operations in the Middle East.

“Donald Trump and his administration are endangering the lives of servicemembers and spending billions on an illegal war that is not supported by the majority of Americans,” Schumer said in a press release. “The 60-day milestone underscores the pressing need to pass our War Powers Resolution to withdraw forces from this war, but to be clear – Congress should have acted weeks ago.”

“Senate Republicans must finally stand up for American families and hold Trump accountable,” he added.

The press conference is scheduled to begin at 10 a.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Saturday's shooting demands reflection from the media, and all of us
data.description:
The White House Correspondents’ Association dinner is supposed to represent the First Amendment and the role of a free press in our democracy. On Saturday, it came to symbolize something darker. As the president was rushed off stage and journalists dove under tables, the country saw the end result of a culture that some of the institutions in that room had helped create.

Of course, neither the press nor the politicians in attendance intentionally stoked the political violence gripping America. But they have fed the public narratives so apocalyptic that unstable people see violence as justified. And too often, the rest of us have rewarded those narratives with our clicks and votes.

On the far left, Americans are told that their democratically elected leaders are fascists who wish to erase marginalized populations. Between 2022 and 2024, MSNBC mentioned President Trump alongside “fascism” 3,032 times.

On the far right, they are told that deep state traitors are seeking to replace the American people with a hoard of invaders. Tucker Carlson alone has invoked the “Great Replacement” theory in more than 400 episodes.

These narratives don’t stay contained. They are reinforced by politicians hungry for attention, and they sow seeds of glory and retribution in fragile minds. Read the manifesto of Saturday’s shooter, and it becomes clear that he viewed himself as a hero, a self-styled “friendly federal assassin” doing the people’s work.

This should have been cause for pause in the media, but it was not. The very next morning, Norah O’Donnell of “60 Minutes” asked Trump to respond to the shooter’s most inflammatory accusations, including that the president is a “pedophile, rapist, and traitor.” What more could an unstable person ask for than to have his words put to the president on national television?

Instead of platforming the shooter and his smears, the instinct should have been to interrogate the political sickness that produced his actions. That could have involved pointed questions to President Trump about his own rhetoric, but it also demanded some introspection from journalists themselves.

If the media and political elite will not take the cue, then the American people must.

We must recognize our own role. The wave of political violence on both sides is the end result of an ideological poison supplied by political extremes and distributed by many in the media. But for them to stop spreading it, we must stop consuming it. That means calling out their narratives as lies — versions of reality so filtered in darkness that they are no longer reality at all.

The vast political center in America understands this intuitively. They know the stories told on TV do not correspond to what they see in their neighborhoods. They desperately want normalcy and goodness to prevail in our discourse. Yet too often, they put the “silent” in silent majority.

But they are, indeed, a majority — consisting of not just the historic number of registered Independents but also the center across both parties. It is time for these Americans to form a political movement of their own. Even just by changing what they click on and vote for, they could alter the incentives that drive our media and political parties.

Without such a movement, the wave of political violence will likely continue. No media institution or political leader intended Saturday’s shooting to happen, nor do they recognize their role in stopping the next one.

We do not need more hollow calls for unity from those who have no intention of changing. What we need is a cultural reset: a media that covers the extremes without amplifying them, politicians who compete on ideas without declaring opponents evil, and a citizen movement strong enough to insist on both.

Nancy Jacobson is a co-founder and CEO of No Labels.

data.title: Trump: Comey knows 'full well' that '86' is 'mob term for kill'
data.description:
President Trump on Wednesday accused former FBI Director James Comey of knowing “full well” that the phrase “86” is a “mob term for kill him.”

Comey was indicted in a case brought by the Department of Justice that is related to a social media post from last year in which Comey included a photo of seashells arranged on a beach that spelled out “86 47.” Trump is the 47th president of the U.S.

The former FBI agent, a longtime antagonist of the president who Trump fired during his first term, faces two charges for allegedly threatening Trump with the seashells post, according to the unsealed indictment.

Comey has said he is innoncent of the charges and that he did not realize the image could have been interpreted as advocating violence against Trump. He took down the post when told of that implication.

But Trump has pressed for the Department of Justice to prosecute Comey, and he argued Wednesday that the ex-FBI leader knew what he was doing.

“‘86’ is a mob term for ‘kill him.’ They say 86 him! 86 47 means ‘kill President Trump,’” the president wrote in a Wednesday post on Truth Social. 

“James Comey, who is a Dirty Cop, one of the worst, knows this full well! EIGHT MILES OUT, SIX FEET DOWN! Didn’t he also lie to the FBI about this??? I think so!” he added.

In a recent interview, when asked if the post endangered his life, Trump said, “Probably, I don’t know, you know, based on what I’m seeing out there, yeah.”

“The people like Comey have created tremendous danger, I think, for politicians and others,” he added, in response to the question from CNN’s Kaitlan Collins. 

The case brought by DOJ has come under scrutiny from a number of voices, however, who have said its merits are week.

Rep. Troy Nehls (R-Texas), a fervent Trump backer, told reporters at the Capitol the case was “a stretch.”

Law professor Jonathan Turley wrote an op-ed for Fox News in which he asserted that the indictment was “facially unconstitutional absent some unknown new facts.”

The case was brought in the wake of a third assassination attempt on Trump, this time while he was attending the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner at the Washington Hilton on Saturday night. 

The suspect intended to target the president and Trump administration officials, according to a manifesto obtained by police. 

Following the indictment, Comey, who made his first court appearance on Wednesday, has declared his innocence publicly and denied wrongdoing. 

“I’m still innocent, I’m still not afraid, and I still believe in the independent federal judiciary. So let’s go,” Comey said in a clip posted to Substack. 

Comey previously faced false statements and obstruction charges stemming from 2020 testimony he gave Congress concerning leaks at the FBI. However, the case was dismissed over the unlawful appointment of the prosecutor who pursued them.

Still, Trump on Wednesday described the ex-FBI director as a “dirty cop” and “crooked man.”

data.title: Brent crude oil hits 4-year high on Iran worries
data.description:
Global oil prices surged to a four-year high overnight before retreating somewhat early Thursday as the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, and the prospect of a deal to end the conflict looks dim.

The price of Brent crude oil, the international benchmark, briefly jumped to $126 per barrel on Thursday — its highest level both during the war and since 2022. Futures fell on Thursday morning, with Brent crude trading around $108 per barrel as of 8:30 a.m. EDT.

The volatility reflects growing concern about how long dual U.S. and Iranian blockades in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical shipping channel in the Gulf that has been effectively closed to shipping traffic since the start of the conflict, could last.

Only a few vessels have managed to get through the waterway over the past two months, intensifying fears of prolonged supply disruptions and inflationary market pressure. About one-fifth of the world’s oil supply is transported through the strait daily during peacetime.

Markets reacted sharply after Axios reported on Wednesday that President Trump was set to be briefed on plans for potentially resuming the U.S. military operation in Iran.

The outlet, citing several sources with knowledge of the matter, noted that U.S. Central Command has prepared a plan for a “short and powerful” wave of strikes on Iran in hopes of bringing Tehran to the negotiating table.

Iranian officials have so far refused to commit to another round of face-to-face talks with U.S. representatives in Pakistan, casting doubt on the potential for a swift, permanent end to hostilities.

In the meantime, Trump has ordered U.S. forces to maintain their naval blockade in the Strait of Hormuz. He told reporters in the Oval Office on Wednesday that the blockade was “genius” and “100 percent foolproof.”

“We’ve got an amazing military. Now they have to cry uncle. That’s all they have to do. Just say, ‘we give up,’” the president said.

The strait has emerged as Iran’s main point of leverage in the war, with its leaders seeking to inflict enough economic pain to secure concessions from the Trump administration.

The regime has reportedly offered to loosen its grip on the strait in exchange for the U.S. lifting its blockade but wants to defer discussions about its nuclear program to a later date.

Trump and Secretary of State Marco Rubio have dismissed that option as a likely nonstarter, even as they face the squeeze of rising energy prices at home.

The national average for a gallon of gas stood at $4.30 on Thursday, according to AAA. Averages topped $5 per gallon in at least four states — Hawaii, Nevada, Oregon and Washington — while prices in California stand at roughly $6 per gallon.

Patrick De Haan, the head of petroleum analysis at GasBuddy, said on Wednesday that gas prices in Michigan, Indiana, Ohio, Illinois and Wisconsin have reached their highest levels since summer 2022 and are nearing all-time records.

data.title: Inflation spikes to 3.5 percent in March as Iran war drove prices higher
data.description:
Prices grew at a far more rapid rate in March as the war in Iran drove a significant increase in inflation, according to data released Thursday by the Commerce Department.

The annual inflation rate in March rose to 3.5 percent, as measured by the personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index, up from 2.8 percent in February. Without food and energy prices, which have spiked amid the war in Iran, the annual inflation rate last month was 3.2 percent.

Prices also jumped 0.7 percent last month alone, up from a 0.4 percent increase in February, according to the Commerce Department.

The PCE price index is the Federal Reserve’s preferred measure of inflation. The March annual inflation rate of 3.5 percent is far above the Fed’s target of 2 percent annual inflation.

The new inflation data was in line with the expectations of economists, who have warned about the steep toll the Iran war could take on the U.S. economy.

President Trump and Republicans, who are already facing intense criticism over their handling of the economy, are likely to face even more pressure from the spike in price growth.

Developing

data.title: Mills suspends Senate campaign in Maine
data.description:
Maine Gov. Janet Mills (D) announced Thursday that she will suspend her Senate campaign.

“When I decided to run for the United States Senate last year, it was because I believed Maine people were getting a bad deal from Washington and because the President of the United States was threatening our democracy and pushing our nation to the brink of disaster,” Mills said in a Thursday statement from her campaign. “I continue to believe that today.”

“While I have the drive and passion, commitment and experience, and above all else — the fight — to continue on, I very simply do not have the one thing that political campaigns unfortunately require today: the financial resources,” she continued. “That is why today I have made the incredibly difficult decision to suspend my campaign for the United States Senate.”

Mills, who is term limited as governor, jumped into the race late last year to challenge incumbent Sen. Susan Collins (R), with support from Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) and other left-leaning groups.

Developing.

data.title: Abortion is an economic issue Republicans aren't ready to face
data.description:
Republicans are wildly out of touch with the American people when it comes to abortion access, so now they’ve decided to just stop talking about it.

New research from Reproductive Freedom for All shows why that won’t work — voters connect reproductive freedom to the economic pressures that weigh on their shoulders every day.  

Planning a family is one of the biggest personal and financial decisions a person will make in their lifetime. New research shows that 82 percent of voters say it has gotten harder to afford to raise children compared to five or ten years ago — and more than half say it is much harder. Nearly three in four say their personal financial situation is a major factor in deciding whether to have a child.  

The costs they name are straightforward: housing, childcare, healthcare, education. People are delaying starting families because they are trying to build the stability needed to take on that responsibility — finishing school, establishing their careers, and creating a foundation for the future.

Voters understand that deciding when to have a child is deliberate. Reproductive freedom — from access to contraception and family planning to paid leave policies that let parents stay in the workforce after having a child — is what allows people to build a solid foundation.

For too long, candidates have been told they have to pick a lane: talk about the economy or talk about abortion. But voters are not picking lanes.

In recent focus groups with women in battleground states, participants brought up abortion on their own, without prompting, when discussing the economy, the cost of living, and life as a woman in America. They talked about what happens in a medical emergency, about fear of losing access to care, and about feeling completely unsupported as mothers. And they talked about all of it alongside the rising costs of groceries, healthcare, and surviving on multiple jobs.

Furthermore, Navigator Research found that women in battleground states are making decisions about whether and when to have children based on whether they can build financial stability — and access to a full range of reproductive healthcare, including contraception, is how they stay in control of that timeline.

Meanwhile, President Trump, Vice President JD Vance and their allies have spent their time in power working to make the economic hardships people face even worse — particularly those who are raising kids. An administration that calls for more babies while gutting the healthcare, childcare and economic support that families need to survive is not pro-family. Just a few weeks ago, Trump said the U.S. should stop funding daycare and pour that money instead into war with Iran.

States with abortion bans and extreme restrictions are costing the national economy more than $64 billion a year according to one analysis — money which, under truly pro-family policies, would go toward alleviating the costs of pregnancy, childbirth and postpartum care in the U.S.

Meanwhile, enhanced Affordable Care Act tax credits expired in January, and premiums increased by an average of 114 percent for the 22 million Americans who depend on marketplace coverage. Nearly 5 million are projected to lose coverage entirely this year. Medicaid, which covers 40 percent of all births in this country, is on the chopping block. And yet some Republicans have even suggested further cuts to healthcare may be coming. 

Last month, Sen. Josh Hawley introduced a bill to revoke FDA approval of the most widely used abortion medication in the country, mifepristone. Vulnerable House Republicans scrambled to distance themselves, with one answering a single “No” when asked whether the party should focus on abortion before November. They know they are losing, they know being anti-abortion is often disqualifying to voters and they know they cannot untangle abortion from the affordability crisis this administration has stoked.

When abortion is on the ballot, we win. Voters understand what is at stake and how reproductive freedom and the economy are connected. They know that they need a strong economic foundation to be able to take on the most important responsibility there is: making decisions about their family.

Eight in 10 Americans support legal abortion. The question for candidates in 2026 is whether they will meet people where they are or continue to ignore an issue that voters see as central to their lives.

Mini Timmaraju is president and CEO of Reproductive Freedom for All, which mobilizes more than 4 million members to fight for reproductive freedom. Molly Murphy is president of Impact Research, which provides insights at the forefront of political and corporate action.
